summary: DeVault responds to Armin Ronacher's argument that Prusa's struggles justify non-free licensing by arguing the real problem is business strategy, not open source. Prusa failed to diversify beyond selling a single premium printer line, leaving massive market opportunities untapped while Chinese competitors like Bambu and Creality offered better products at lower prices. DeVault argues Prusa should have built an ecosystem — entry-level printers, cloud services, a model marketplace, consumables pipeline — rather than blaming open source for their decline. He concludes that FOSS is a powerful commercial tool, but businesses must do the hard work of sales, marketing, and risk mitigation rather than assuming the product alone is sufficient.
key_points: Prusa's non-free firmware move is immaterial to their competitive struggle — Bambu respects copyleft and doesn't use Prusa's code
Chinese manufacturing competition was always the obvious risk, and Prusa failed to prepare by diversifying their business model beyond selling printers
Prusa left massive market opportunities untapped: entry-level printers, cloud slicing, on-demand printing, model marketplaces, and consumables subscriptions
The product itself is maybe 10% of the work — FOSS makes that 10% community property, but you still need to do the other 90% of building a business
Blaming open source for business failures caused by strategic negligence is a misdiagnosis that leads to the wrong prescription (restrictive licensing)
money_quotes: Prusa made one of the classic errors in open source business models: they made their product, made it open source, sold it, and assumed that they were done working on their business model.
Maybe 10% of the work is developing the 3D printer itself – don't abandon the other 90% of your business.
Especially when you make that 10% open: someone else is going to repurpose it, do the other 90%, and eat your lunch.
FOSS is great precisely because it makes that 10% into community property and shares the cost of innovation, but you'd be a fool to act as if that was all there was to it.
Open source is not an 'if you build it, the money will come' situation, and to think otherwise is a grave mistake.
These are not cheap knock-offs: if you want the best 3D printer today it's going to be a Chinese one, regardless of how much you want to spend, but, as it happens, you're going to spend less.
This is a classic failure mode of open source businesses, and it's Prusa's fault.
key_insight: Prusa's decline isn't a failure of open source licensing but a failure of business strategy — they built a great product and stopped there, neglecting the 90% of business-building that has nothing to do with the product itself.
